---
title: "*Can You Tell an Azeotrope from a Peritectic? Get Your Phase Diagrams Right!* (Reading Phase Diagrams) Reference Page"
author: "DOFPro group"
format: html
html-math-method: mathjax
---
::: {.callout-important title="Still Under Construction!"}
This web page is just a bare outline. The complete details have not been filled in yet.
:::
# Intro to *Can You Tell an Azeotrope from a Peritectic? Get Your Phase Diagrams Right!*

Main title explanation

## Part 1

Video 1 explanation

<iframe width="560" height="315" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/NUS4Y_pnWZk?si=As2g0ermI338GBaa" title="YouTube video player" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen></iframe>
[Visuals](../../NotYetHere.qmd)

## Part 2

Video  explanation

<iframe width="560" height="315" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/QBREikNSvGI?si=SyJVNz-QI7cnDjpa" title="YouTube video player" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen></iframe>
[Visuals](../../NotYetHere.qmd)

# Examples and Definitions

## Examples Links

## Definitions

Phase Diagram
: A graphical representation of the equilibrium between different phases of a substance as a function of temperature, pressure, and composition. Phase diagrams are used to understand the conditions under which different phases of a substance coexist and to predict the behavior of a substance under various conditions. They are commonly used in materials science, chemistry, and chemical engineering to analyze phase transitions, such as melting, boiling, and sublimation, as well as to design processes and materials with specific properties.

Azeotrope
: A mixture of two or more liquids that has a constant boiling point and composition throughout the distillation process. Azeotropes occur when the vapor phase has the same composition as the liquid phase at a specific temperature and pressure, resulting in a unique point on the phase diagram. Azeotropes can be classified as minimum boiling azeotropes, which have a lower boiling point than either of the pure components, or maximum boiling azeotropes, which have a higher boiling point than either of the pure components. Azeotropes are important in chemical engineering and separation processes, as they can limit the effectiveness of distillation and require special techniques for separation.

Vapor
: The gas phase of a substance below its critical temperature but above its vapor pressure.

Gas
: The gas phase of a substance above its critical temperature. It is sometimes used to refer to vapors as well.

Triple Point
: The unique combination of temperature and pressure at which three phases of a substance coexist in equilibrium. The triple point is a fundamental property of a substance and is used to define the standard temperature and pressure (STP) conditions. At the triple point, the substance can exist simultaneously as a solid, liquid, and gas, and the phase diagram will show a point where the three phase boundaries intersect.

Lever Rule
: A graphical method used to determine the relative amounts of different phases in a two-phase region of a phase diagram. The lever rule is based on the principle of mass balance and is applied by drawing a tie line across the two-phase region at the desired composition. The lengths of the segments of the tie line on either side of the composition point are used to calculate the fraction of each phase present in the mixture. The lever rule is a useful tool for analyzing phase diagrams and understanding the behavior of mixtures in various applications, such as in materials science and chemical engineering.

Eutectic
: A liquid phase transforms into two solid phases upon cooling.

Eutectoid
: A solid phase transforms into two solid phases upon cooling.

Peritectic
: A liquid phase and a solid phase combine to form a new solid phase upon cooling.

Peritectoid
: Two solid phases combine to form a new solid phase upon cooling.

Monotectic
: A liquid phase transforms into a solid phase and a new liquid phase upon cooling.

Syntectic
: Two liquid phases react to form a solid phase upon cooling.

# Other Links and Videos

## Other Links

## Previous and Following Videos
